captions.text: Super Meat Boy 3D is, to skip to the final  summary, a version of Super Meat Boy but in
3D. And I have to say it made me very nostalgic  for the era when sticking "3D" on the end of a
title just meant that you had to put on special  glasses to look at it. I wish Super Meat Boy 3D
had come with special glasses. Maybe with  completely blacked out lenses or little
pictures of characters from better games.  When Super Meat Boy 3D was first announced,
I and all of my colleagues immediately agreed  that it looked extremely ill-advised. And yet,
I played it anyway. "Huh, looks like there's a  train on the horizon; something tells me it's
going to wreck itself. Huh, that's funny, it  seems to be coming straight towards me. Huh,
that's funny, it doesn't seem to be on a rail.  Or on the ground." And now I find myself standing
amid the ruins of this train, saying to myself,  "Yep, figured as much. Guess I'll go write this
all up for the transit disaster enthusiast press  as soon as I've sewn my legs back on." What should
probably be communicated up front is that Meat  Boy creator Edmund McMillen had nothing to do
with this; he's been too busy feeding stray  cats to woodchippers for the last few years,
so rest assured he has an alibi placing  him far from the scene of the accident.
And you can kinda tell from the complete absence  of his personality in this game—or indeed any
personality at all. It's more or less a copy-paste  of the Super Meat Boy format—fast-paced precision
platformer, map screen, level by level, dark  world, boss levels, you play a red blob trying
to rescue a pink blob from another pink blob—but  all with the air of a plane in a holding pattern
being desperately controlled by an inexperienced  co-pilot after the captain bailed out with all
the parachutes and fuel. So when the trailer for  this came out, one of the things brought up by
us game review veterans from a previous era when  things like expertise and common sense actually
fucking mattered was that precision platforming  is exponentially harder to pull off in 3D and even
more so with fixed cameras because determining  where you are in any given moment relative to
the environment is fine when you only have  left, right, up, and down to worry about,
but it gets a lot harder when in and out is a  factor. And that's not the only thing. Am I right,
ladies? "Oh, perish the thought, Yahtz, that  our notoriously and knowingly super hard game
should be super hard." Don't you fucking smart  mouth me, Meat Boy. I know your fucking dad.
"Hard but fair" is the phrase, "BUT FAIR" being  the operative words, and your butt is very
far from fair. The other thing 2D does well is  letting you know when you're going out of bounds
on account of the edges of the screen letting  you know that no more map physically exists,
whereas in this, you're hopping around looking for  shortcuts and suddenly explode when you reach a
point that the game arbitrarily decided wasn't  part of the map. So it plays like fried shite,
but you all figured that out 16 frames into the  gameplay trailer; what's really worth harping
on—not to mention timely—is just how horrible it  looks. It's literally the DLSS 5 meme in action,
isn't it? On the left side is the original Super  Meat Boy with its 2D animation full of life and
charm and energy and expression, and on the  right is Super Meat Boy 3D with its dead eyes
and realistic lighting putting ugly graded  shadows over everything and the characters
covered in high-definition textured skin that  looks like somebody's ball sack. And not for
the first time we ask ourselves: who, besides  Disney's live action remake division and the
apparent millions of filmgoers that make them  profitable, think that this is an improvement?
I mean, call me a dreary, old, alienated  snob who smells weird and murders people
in an abandoned nail factory, but at what  point does worse gameplay, less appealing
characters, alienating lower-income audiences,  corporate-engineered inequality, climate disaster,
and the eventual breakdown of Western civilization  become too high a price to pay for nicer graphics
cards? For making Grace Ashcroft look marginally  more like a real person in the brief moments when
her face isn't either in or pointing away from  the camera? It's an interesting counterpoint
to another sequel to a classic indie game that  I've been playing this week—yeah, that's right,
stealth double review, which I'm doing as  an intriguing surprise and not because the
algorithm really shits on double bills for some  reason—Slay the Spire 2. Still in early access,
hence the stealth double review loophole, but  there's plenty of meat to get one's teeth into,
even if some of it is a cardboard cutout of  some meat with the word "placeholder" written
around it in garlic butter sauce. It's a  straightforward deckbuilder in which you
proceed through a ladder of randomized battles  and encounters, acquiring and modifying cards
in the vain hope that maybe this time you'll  craft yourself a suitably blatantly unfairly
overpowered build with which to tackle the  similarly blatantly overpowered final boss.
And I was a little thrown by how massively  successful the early access build has been
since its release. Yes, it's a sequel to one  of the original indie roguelike deckbuilders,
but surely the genre has moved on since then,  producing more advanced deckbuilders like
Marvel Snap or that one where you're a train.  And acknowledging that half the images on the
lore timeline are hasty MS Paint scribbles 'cause  it's early access, even the finished art seems a
bit overly simple. Like, you'll play a card  called something like "Quadruple Somersault
Chainsaw Juggle Fireworks Party," and all your  dude does is weakly stab the air in front of him
with a lame tweened animation while an admittedly  impressive number flies through the air. And I
guess my conclusion after playing the shit out of  it for a few days is that its simplicity is why
it's so appealing. In much the same way people  still play the original Doom because you can
actually tell what's going on at a glance without  being muddied by realistic shadows and you can run
the game on the fucking digital clock on the front  of a microwave from the 1980s. Slay the Spire is a
largely numbers-based experience, and very little  gets in the way of that. It's always perfectly
clear what numbers you're about to produce and  what numbers are going to come out of the enemy
in response; it's like two competing judging  panels at the Olympic figure skating event.
Slay the Spire 2 adds a co-op multiplayer mode,  which doesn't count for much with me as you might
expect, although I did take time to play it  with m'colleague Jack Packard for a spell and
didn't have an awful time despite the hernia I  acquired carrying his crapout ass. There's also
new playable characters, my favorite being  the necromancer lady who can inflict Doom,
which is a special number that instant kills an  enemy at the end of their turn if their health
number goes below it. And it's very fucking  satisfying. It's like Fist of the North Star
for accountants. "You are already dead."  Digs out calculator; bip boop beep bop. "Oh,
shit! It adds up! BLEH." But besides that,  I did have a concern that Slay the Spire 2
hadn't evolved much from the original until  I thought to actually play the original and
was surprised by how much visual polish had  in fact been added that I hadn't noticed.
And that's why Slay the Spire 2 is the  perfect counterpoint to Super Meat Boy 3D:
because it puts me in a lovely, comfortable,  reassuring place by adding just enough polish
to evoke how my nostalgic memory remembered  the original. Whereas Super Meat Boy 3D does
the equivalent of drowning the original game  to death face down in a pool of Brasso. Hang
on. Am I forgetting something? Oh, yeah. I  don't really murder people in a nail factory.
